Oh, I see that ``urlparse()`` splitted the part after `/` into this `path` attribute.
So this has never worked.

```
>>> import testinfra
>>> backend = testinfra.get_host('kubectl://pod/c').backend
>>> backend.name
'pod'
>>> backend.container is None
True
```

In this case I think you can just drop this feature of splitting the container and the pod name with `/` and just rely on url querystring.

A good test could be:

````
b = testinfra.get_host('kubectl://pod?namespace=space&container=nginx').backend
assert b.name == 'pod'
assert b.namespace == 'space'
assert b.container == 'nginx'
```

Also I think this deserve some documentation in `doc/source/backend.rst` ;)

Yes, to address the same race that Julien pointed out in the context of setting _txn_time in https://github.com/zopefoundation/ZODB/pull/56

If a connection called lastTransaction() after the cache tid was set but before getting the invalidations, then it would proceed with the transaction without having properly invalidating the cache.

This PR mitigates this race in 2 ways:

1. It gets the storage lock in ``lastTransactuon``.

2. It sets the cache tid (and thus the lastTransaction result) after sending invalidations.  The race is unlikely, but if we lose, we lose safely by using a transaction start time that's too early.

> Don’t use /etc/ssl/certs/ca-bundle.crt, even though it does probably exist on most current Fedora/RHEL installs; it’s not a canonical location for Fedora/RHEL and it’s unlikely to exist on anything else, and it may possibly go away on Fedora in future. Just don’t use it.

> So, if you wanted to handle trust store locations yourself, you could probably cover all or most Linux distros by checking for /etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt and /etc/ssl/certs in that order and using the first found, in the appropriate way (the first as a bundle file, the second as a directory). But you still wouldn’t be covering non-platform builds of OpenSSL, or OS X, or Windows.

So this doesn't quite work. The idea behind proof of possession is that the server gives the client a public key and the client has to sign a message using the corresponding private key.

`self._gen_response` simply takes a key and signs the message with no check that the correct private key was used. The result is that the code currently grabs the first private key it finds in `key_dir` and uses it to sign the message. What you need to do is load each private key as a JSON web key, get the corresponding public key, and compare it with the key given by the server. Only when you find a match can you generate a response and return it.

To do this nicely will require a bit of refactoring. Basically what you want to do is factor out the code that loads the key in `_gen_response` into a new function. You can then use this function both in `_gen_response` and in the code above to return a JWK object. You can then call `.public_key()` on that object to return the public key. After that, you need to compare it `achall.hints.jwk`. If there is a match, use the already loaded in key to generate the response.

Hopefully that all makes sense. Please follow up if you have any questions.

It's a try/except/else branch so the `if np.issubdtype(dtypes[field], np.integer)` block runs only if the try has already *succeeded*:
```python
try:
    dtypes[field] = ty.to_numpy_dtype()
except TypeError:
    dtypes[field] = np.dtype(object)
else:
    # If `ty.to_numpy_dtype()` results in an integer type override `dtypes[field]` to be float64
    # We do this because we already know `dtype` is an `Option` type and pandas uses NaN
    # as a null placeholder requiring that the type be floating point rather than integral
    if np.issubdtype(dtypes[field], np.integer):
        # cast nullable ints to float64 so NaN can be used for nulls
        dtypes[field] = np.float64
```

One benefit is an improved ability to re-use test methods. See an example [here](https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/pull/126/files#r53498450). In short, many of these test methods contain highly similar logic, and pushing that logic into `setUpClass` lets us create more reusable test methods.

A second benefit is assertion isolation. For example, given this code:

```python
self.assertEqual(tags_in_src, tags_in_dest)
self.assertEqual(manifests_in_src, manifests_in_dest)
```

...if the first assertion fails, the second will not run, which is likely undesirable. Given that we have only one assertion in this test method, it does not apply in this particular case.

A third benefit is an increased ability to add or remove assertions about a particular outcome. Talking to the server and saving the munged responses just once means that we can write as many assertions as we want targeting the response(s).

One downside with this approach is that this received Data Request message is wasted and cannot trigger delivery of a pending Data Frame.

A more effective approach might be to simply enable short source address matching after the first Indirect Transmission attempt to the child.  This will result in the following two cases:
1. The Child ID Response made it to the child.  The child will use its short address in the next Data Request frame.
2. The Child ID Response did not make it to the child.  The child will need to send a new Child ID Request anyway.

In this way, we never have a situation where we "waste" a Data Request message.

This is a really bad idea IMO, it's adding a hidden contract, were for the filters to work properly the rpc client `block_number` method must be used, otherwise the cache is not updated and new events will not be polled.

For this to work properly this is required:

1. Because the cache is only updated on calls to `JSONRPCClient.block_number`, this method must be called
2. For the above, we rely on [`BlockChainService.block_number()`](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/6444580a4208a32afcebc5415c4c34db671967c2/raiden/network/blockchain_service.py#L49-L50) to always forward the call to it's client instance.
3. For the above to happen, we rely on the [alarm task to call chain.block_number()](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/6444580a4208a32afcebc5415c4c34db671967c2/raiden/tasks.py#L79)

If we change 2 or 3 (e.g. replace by a direct call to the web3) the cache will not be updated and the filter will stop working, if this happens it will be a hard to debug problem.
